canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, COMET (Crosslingual Optimized Metric for Evaluation of Translation) is consistently characterized as a learned evaluation metric that employs neural models to assess translation quality. The most prevalent definition describes it as using multilingual representations, with several papers specifying that it evaluates aspects like "semantic adequacy" and "fluency." A less common framing describes it more broadly as a metric for scoring the quality of any generated text relative to a reference. In practice, COMET is deployed as a standard evaluation metric for translation tasks, frequently used alongside BLEU and BERTScore. One study notes the use of a specific version, "wmt22-comet-da," while another reports its results as a percentage. Its performance relative to other metrics is a point of discussion; one paper states that "COMET shows similar results as BLEU," while another finds that it "exhibits a stronger correlation with idiom translation compared to BLEU and BERTScore."
expert_removed_reason: metric/method/tool — not a benchmark or LLM ability
papers: A Case Against Implicit Standards: Homophone Normalization in Machine Translation for Languages that use theGe’ez Script.

Enhancing Large Vision-Language Models with Ultra-Detailed Image Caption Generation
Measuring Risk of Bias in Biomedical Reports: TheRoBBRBenchmark
TALON: A Multi-Agent Framework for Long-Table Exploration and Question Answering
Towards General-Domain Word Sense Disambiguation: Distilling Large Language Model into Compact Disambiguator
XAutoLM: Efficient Fine-Tuning of Language Models via Meta-Learning andAutoML
ScalingLLMInference Efficiently with Optimized Sample Compute Allocation
VisDoM: Multi-DocumentQAwith Visually Rich Elements Using Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation

definitions.text: Crosslingual Optimized Metric for Evaluation of Translation, a learned evaluation metric that uses neural models to assess translation quality based on multilingual representations.

characterization: Across the provided literature, COMET (Crosslingual Optimized Metric for Evaluation of Translation) is consistently characterized as a learned evaluation metric that employs neural models to assess translation quality. The most prevalent definition describes it as using multilingual representations, with several papers specifying that it evaluates aspects like "semantic adequacy" and "fluency." A less common framing describes it more broadly as a metric for scoring the quality of any generated text relative to a reference. In practice, COMET is deployed as a standard evaluation metric for translation tasks, frequently used alongside BLEU and BERTScore. One study notes the use of a specific version, "wmt22-comet-da," while another reports its results as a percentage. Its performance relative to other metrics is a point of discussion; one paper states that "COMET shows similar results as BLEU," while another finds that it "exhibits a stronger correlation with idiom translation compared to BLEU and BERTScore."
